about.title: What Is Blood Donation & Why It Matters

card1.title: Save Lives
card1.body: One donation can save up to 3 lives. Since blood cannot be manufactured artificially, donation is the only source.

card2.title: Support Healthcare
card2.body: Cancer treatment, surgery, childbirth — around 5 million people need blood transfusions each year. Donation supports frontline medicine.

card3.title: Free Health Check
card3.body: After donating, you receive your blood test results. It's also a free opportunity to check your own health status.

info.title: Basic Information
info.reqHealth: Good health on the day of donation

faqItems.q: Is it okay if it's my first time?
faqItems.a: Yes, staff will guide you carefully.
There may be slight pain when the needle is inserted, but it's brief.
Please avoid strenuous exercise on the day of donation.

info.appLinksTitle: Reserve ahead with the Labrad app
info.appLinksText: Use it for blood donation card management and advance reservations.
info.sponsor_value: Osaka Sonezaki Lions Club / Osaka Nishi Lions Club
info.reservation_value: Making a reservation will shorten the time required for blood donation procedures.
info.gift_value: Those who cooperate with blood donation will receive a 'commemorative gift' from the Lions Club.

precautions.title: Things to Check Before Participating
precautions.intro: These are not strict rules — they're to protect your own health. If you have any concerns, feel free to ask staff on the day.
eligibilityItems: Ages 16 to 69 (65+ requires prior donation experience)
Good health and adequate sleep on the day
precautions.dayOfTitle: On the day, this is fine
precautions.dayOfNote: Get enough sleep and eat before coming. If you have a fever or feel unwell, please refrain from participating.
dayOfItems: Eat before coming (donating on an empty stomach is prohibited)
Drink plenty of water or sports drinks
Don't push yourself if you feel unwell
prohibitedItems: You have a fever or cold symptoms
You drank heavily the night before
You recently had surgery, a transfusion, or a tattoo

fs.title: For International Students & Foreign Nationals
fs.subtitle: Eligibility conditions vary by nationality and region of origin. Please check before registering.
fs.r1_body: As long as you reside legally in Japan and can answer the medical interview in Japanese, you are welcome to participate!
fs.r2_title: Residing in Japan for over 4 weeks
fs.r2_body: No matter where you are from, as long as you have been in Japan for more than 4 weeks, you can register to donate.
fs.r3_body: If you have lived in rural or highland areas with malaria risks, you may need to wait 1-3 years after returning to Japan.
fs.tableNote: ※ This is a general guide only. Please confirm details with staff on the day.
fs.vn_urban: Hanoi, Ho Chi Minh City & major cities
fs.eu_all: UK, France, Germany, etc. (restrictions easing from autumn 2026)
fs.langBody: The questionnaire and doctor interview are in Japanese. If you're unsure, please ask staff in advance.
fs.ctaBody: If you're not sure whether you're eligible to donate, feel free to ask staff before registering or on the day.

lastYear.title: Here's how our 2025 campus blood drive went.
lastYear.summary: On September 24, 2025, the event was held in the 1st floor lounge of Building 1 at ECC Computer College. Over 30 students and faculty participated, with student volunteers playing an active role.
captions: The 1st floor lounge of Building 1, the event venue.
The blood donation bus parked next to Building 1, in front of Building 4.
Up to 3 donors at a time inside the bus. Radio played softly — a relaxed atmosphere.
During donation, reading the precautions pamphlet and receiving tips from nurses.
Student volunteers handled pre-registration check-in and guided attendees.
Student volunteers joined Lions Club members to encourage blood donation.
lastYear.sourceLink: ECC Social Action Center Activity Report

knowledge.sectionTitle: Knowing about blood donation is the first step to saving lives.
knowledge.sectionBody: Blood donation isn't just for special people. With the right knowledge, anxiety fades, and everyone — whether they donate or share the word — becomes part of supporting local healthcare.
knowledge.seeContributions: See what blood donation can contribute

cards.title: Blood Donation: Society's Medical Infrastructure
cards.text: Surgery, heavy bleeding in childbirth, cancer treatment — situations requiring transfusions arise daily. Blood cannot be manufactured artificially or stored long-term.
cards.detail:
Donated blood has a very short shelf life and cannot be stored long-term.
- Red blood cells: Lasts up to 21 days
- Platelets: Lasts only 4 days
Because blood cannot be artificially manufactured, steady and continuous donations from the community are the only way to support daily emergency surgeries and cancer treatments in hospitals.

cards.title: One Donation Reaches Multiple Patients
cards.text: Donated blood is separated by component — red cells, platelets, plasma — and delivered to patients who need each part.
cards.detail:
Donated blood is separated into three main components using centrifuge technology to help different patients:
- Red blood cells: Used for surgeries and trauma emergency transfusions
- Platelets: Used to stop bleeding in patients with leukemia or cancer
- Plasma: Used for treating severe burns and large-scale disasters
Thanks to this advanced separation method, your single donation can save the lives of up to three different patients.

cards.title: Young Participation Protects the Future
cards.text: As the population ages, donors decrease while those needing transfusions increase. Student participation helps sustain local healthcare.
cards.detail:
With Japan's declining birthrate and aging population, securing a stable blood supply is a major challenge.
- Youth participation: The number of teen and 20s donors has halved in the past 20 years
- Transfusion demand: Continues to increase as the elderly population grows
Active participation from students is crucial to preventing a collapse in future local medical services. Even if you cannot donate today, sharing this information helps immensely.

benefits.sectionTitle: Benefits of Donating and Your Contribution to Society
benefits.sectionHelper: Select a card to see a detailed explanation with a photo.

cards.title: A Reason to Check Your Health
cards.text: Registration checks and pre-screening raise your awareness of your own health.
cards.detail: Before donating, your condition, sleep, diet, and blood pressure are reviewed. It's not just about eligibility — it's a valuable opportunity to reflect on your own lifestyle and health.

cards.title: The Feeling of Supporting Life
cards.text: A short act of cooperation becomes a real force supporting someone's treatment or recovery.
cards.detail: Blood cannot be made artificially or stored long-term. Your contribution becomes real, practical support for people who need transfusions in surgery, accidents, childbirth, or cancer treatment.

cards.title: Campus Solidarity
cards.text: Participating with friends and faculty spreads a culture of mutual support.
cards.detail: Going alone can be daunting, but with a friend or teacher it's easier. Approaching it as a campus event creates an atmosphere where 'helping those in need' becomes natural for everyone.

cards.title: Your First Step in Social Contribution
cards.text: No special preparation needed — you can support healthcare right from your campus.
cards.detail: Social contribution doesn't require grand efforts. Participating locally and sharing accurate information with those around you is an important act that sustains community healthcare.

reason.whyBody: Blood cannot be manufactured artificially and cannot be stored for long. That's why daily cooperation is needed to support people who require transfusions for surgery and medical treatment.
reason.processNote: So that first-timers can understand the process, staff will walk you through each step on the day. Feel free to ask questions anytime.
reason.communityBody: Those who can donate do so; those who can't still contribute by sharing information. One person's action spreads compassion across the entire campus.
communityItems: Listen to explanations alongside anxious friends
Build a donation culture that continues next time
reason.imageAlt: Students comfortably participating in a blood donation event

steps.title: Pre-check
steps.detail: Before donation, staff confirm that your condition is suitable for donating safely that day.
checks: Sleep, meals, fever, and current condition
Past donation interval and medication status

steps.title: Interview & test
steps.detail: A short interview and screening help protect both the donor and the patients who receive blood.
checks: Blood pressure and basic condition check

steps.title: Blood donation
steps.detail: During donation, staff stay nearby and monitor your condition. You can speak up immediately if you feel unwell.

pledge.title: Spreading a Blood Donation Culture from Campus
items: Learn the facts and don't carry anxiety alone
Those who can — take the first step; those who can't — spread the word
Make blood donation an ongoing culture of care, not just a one-time event

footerCta.title: Together on campus — let's support blood donation.
footerCta.aiImageNote: Some images on this website are AI-generated illustrations and may differ from the actual venue or people.

stats.save_desc: Blood is split into components to treat multiple patients.
stats.platelet_desc: That's why new blood donations are needed every single day.
stats.annual_desc: Supporting daily medicine like surgeries, cancer, birth, and accidents.

who_needs.title: The Reality of Those Who Need Transfusions
who_needs.desc: "Hospitals" and "surgeries" are not someone else's business. Every day, thousands of people need blood transfusions.

cards.title: Surgery & Emergency
cards.body: Traffic accidents and sudden surgeries require large amounts of blood in a short time. Without blood, lives cannot be saved.

cards.title: Cancer & Blood Diseases
cards.body: In chemotherapy and leukemia treatments, continuous replenishment of platelets and red cells is a prerequisite for treatment.

cards.title: Childbirth & Newborns
cards.body: Heavy bleeding during delivery and transfusions for premature babies are at the very front line of saving lives.

cards.title: Chronic Illness & Long-term Care
cards.body: Patients with kidney disease or aplastic anemia require regular blood transfusions to sustain their ongoing treatments.

steps.title: Reception & Check-up
steps.sub: Confirm health condition and weight (approx. 5 min)

steps.title: Blood Donation
steps.sub: Whole blood collection takes approx. 10–15 min

steps.title: Testing & Separation
steps.sub: Safety confirmation and component separation within 24 hours

steps.title: Delivery to Patients
steps.sub: Sent to hospitals and operating rooms nationwide

trust.label: This event is a safe blood donation activity in partnership with official organizations

faqSection.title: Before participating, let's ease your anxieties
faqSection.subtitle: We answer common questions for those considering blood donation for the first time.
faqSection.q1: Is there any risk of contracting an infection through blood donation?
faqSection.a1: We always use brand new, disposable needles for blood collection. Since a new needle is opened for each person, there is absolutely no contact with anyone else's blood. Please participate with peace of mind.
faqSection.q2: Is the blood really delivered to people who need it?
faqSection.a2: Donated blood is strictly tested and managed at the Japanese Red Cross Society's blood centers. It is reliably delivered to patients who actually need transfusions for surgeries, cancer treatments, childbirth, etc.
faqSection.q3: Will I feel unwell after blood donation?
faqSection.a3: In rare cases, you may feel slightly unwell immediately after donating, but our staff will fully support you. Please eat and sleep well on the day, and rest for 15 to 30 minutes after donating.
faqSection.q4: Is the privacy of the blood donor protected?
faqSection.a4: Blood test results and personal information are strictly managed and will never be provided to third parties. They are handled appropriately based on the Japanese Red Cross Society's personal info protection policy.

eligibility.title: Blood Donation Standards
eligibility.cellVolumePlasma:
600mL or less
(Within 12% of circulating blood volume)
eligibility.cellAgeNote: Note: Donors aged 65-69 are accepted only if they have donated between their 60th birthday and the day before their 65th birthday.
eligibility.cellHemoPlasma:
12.0g/dL or more
(11.5g/dL or more for women with normal RBC indices*)
*MCV: 81-100fL / MCH: 26-35pg / MCHC: 31-36%
eligibility.cellInterval200Val: From the same day of the week 4 weeks later for both
eligibility.cellInterval400Val:
Men: from 12 weeks later,
Women: from 16 weeks later (same weekday)
eligibility.cellInterval400ValPlasma: From the same weekday 8 weeks later for both
eligibility.cellIntervalPlasmaVal: From the same weekday 2 weeks later for both
eligibility.cellIntervalPlateletVal: Possible after 1 week if plasma is not collected. If donated 4 times within a 4-week window, wait 4 weeks until next donation.
eligibility.cellAnnualVolumeVal:
Combining 200mL & 400mL whole blood:
Men: within 1,200mL / Women: within 800mL
eligibility.cellAnnualCount200:
Men: 6 times or less
Women: 4 times or less
eligibility.cellAnnualCount400:
Men: 3 times or less
Women: 2 times or less
eligibility.cellAnnualCountComponent: Within 24 times in total (1 platelet donation counts as 2, combined with plasma)
eligibility.cellCommonVal:
Blood collection is not performed for the following individuals:
1. Pregnant women, or those who were pregnant within the past 6 months.
2. Those with circulatory diseases, blood disorders, or other illnesses that could worsen by donation.
3. Individuals with fever or other poor health conditions.
eligibility.footerNote: * Interval calculations start from the day of the last donation.
